beats.prompt: Cumulative recall. Match each left-hand prompt to its right-hand answer.

feedback.correct: 2ⁿ sums every Pascal row — each step doubles the sum. C(n,k) counts the size-k subsets — equivalently, the number of up-paths to a node with k up-moves. Those nCk counts will weight the risk-neutral node probabilities in the binomial tree.
hints: One card asks about the row sum (what happens when you add the entries of a Pascal row?); one asks what C(n,k) counts.
Pascal's triangle: each row n sums to 2ⁿ because every entry equals the sum of two above it, so row sums double; C(n,k) = number of n-bit strings with exactly k ones = number of size-k subsets.
2ⁿ; size-k subsets (Comb-L3 l3-recall).

beats.prompt: One step priced this call at 10. Now cut the same move into two half-steps — same u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1 each. What is the fair 2-step price of the 100-call?

≈ 14.7.note: Let's test it — a plain average uses weights 1/3, 1/3, 1/3. The risk-neutral weights are 1/4, 1/2, 1/4 (Pascal 1-2-1 over 2²), and you must discount by Rⁿ (here R=1, so no discount). The middle node is twice as likely as each corner, so 1/4·44 = 11, not 44/3.

44.note: Let's test it — those are raw counts, not probabilities; they sum to 4, not 1. Divide by 2ⁿ = 4 (equivalently multiply each by q^k(1−q)^(n−k)): 44/4 = 11. A count becomes a probability only after you normalize.

11.note: Good instinct — pricing a tree is the one-step fold, iterated: C_u=22, C_d=0, then today = ½·22 + ½·0 = 11 (#17).

beats.prompt: Tap any of the 6 nodes in the 2-step binomial lattice. The side panel shows the stock price S, the q-weight nCk·q^k(1−q)^(n−k), and the rolled-back option value at that node. The full backward fold is readable by exploring all nodes.

hero.structuralReadout: 2-step binomial lattice — tap any node to read its stock price, q-weight, and rolled-back option value. Root price: 11.

feedback.correct: Backward fold: terminal nodes S=144→payoff 44; S=96→0; S=64→0. Intermediate layer: C_u = ½·44 + ½·0 = 22; C_d = ½·0 + ½·0 = 0. Root price = ½·22 + ½·0 = 11 (discounted by R=1).
hints: Start at the terminal nodes — their payoffs are max(S−100, 0).
Move to the intermediate layer — C_u = ½·44 + ½·0 = 22; C_d = ½·0 + ½·0 = 0.
Root price = ½·22 + ½·0 = 11 (discounted by R=1).

beats.prompt: Read the model card, then continue.

interaction.title: Three lenses on the binomial price
interaction.body:
Lens 1 — Node weight: nCk·q^k·(1−q)^(n−k). For n=2, q=1/2: k=0 → C(2,0)·(1/4) = 1/4; k=1 → C(2,1)·(1/4) = 1/2; k=2 → C(2,2)·(1/4) = 1/4. Check: 1/4+1/2+1/4 = 1 ✓.

Lens 2 — Price: (1/Rⁿ)·Σ weight·payoff = (1/1)·(1/4·44 + 1/2·0 + 1/4·0) = 11.

Lens 3 — Refutation: drop the (1−q)^(n−k) factor and the weights stop summing to 1. They must sum to 1 — it is a probability distribution over terminals. Always include both powers.

feedback.correct: Node weights nCk·q^k(1−q)^(n−k) sum to 1; price = (1/Rⁿ)·Σ weight·payoff = 11. Omitting (1−q)^(n−k) breaks the sum-to-1 property.
hints: Lens 1: the node weight for k up-moves is nCk·q^k·(1−q)^(n−k). For q=1/2, n=2: weights are 1/4, 1/2, 1/4.
Lens 2: price = (1/Rⁿ)·Σ weight·payoff = 1·(1/4·44 + 1/2·0 + 1/4·0) = 11.
Lens 3: without (1−q)^(n−k), the weights no longer sum to 1 — not a probability distribution.

beats.prompt: Intermediate values are displayed on the lattice: C_u = 22 (up-node) and C_d = 0 (down-node). Using q = 1/2 and R = 1, what is today's fair call price?

feedback.correct: (1/R)·(q·C_u + (1−q)·C_d) = 1·(½·22 + ½·0) = 11. The q-weighted average discounted by R=1 (#17).
hints: The price today is the q-weighted average of C_u and C_d, discounted by R.
q=1/2, R=1, so price = ½·C_u + ½·C_d = ½·22 + ½·0.

beats.prompt: A stock starts at 100; each period it either rises or falls by fixed factors; a risk-free account is available. Two periods. Find today's fair price of the 100-call — which method?

Binomial pricing.note: Yes — the q-weighted backward fold over the lattice, discounting by R at each step. gate.correct = binomial-pricing.

Backward induction.note: Right shape — you do fold from the end. But bare backward induction doesn't carry the risk-neutral q-weights and the 1/R discount; binomial pricing is that fold's q-weighted, discounted specialization.

First-step analysis.note: That conditions forward on the first move and solves a recurrence. Here you fold backward from expiry — the values are known at the leaves, not the root.

Recursion / self-reference.note: Naive recursion re-expands the whole tree (and double-counts the recombining middle node). The structured q-weighted fold visits each node once.

beats.prompt: Drag the σ slider (σ ∈ [0.05, 1.0]). The BS curve morphs as n→∞ (CRR limit, illustrative); the discrete binomial price 11 stays anchored for comparison. As the steps shrink, the binomial price converges to Black-Scholes c = S·N(d₁) − K·e^(−rT)·N(d₂). But N(·) is the normal CDF — irrational, no rational closed form — so the BS number you see (≈ 10.45) is a sourced reference (GB §6.1 L11274/L11360), not a graded answer. The price this lesson grades is the binomial one (11). (Exactly like cov-4 grading ρ² instead of the irrational ρ.)

hero.structuralReadout: σ slider — BS curve morphs toward the binomial price 11; ≈10.45 is a sourced reference, display-only.

feedback.correct: As the steps shrink, the binomial price converges to Black-Scholes c = S·N(d₁) − K·e^(−rT)·N(d₂). N(·) is irrational — so ≈10.45 is a sourced reference, not a graded answer. The graded price is the binomial one: 11.
hints: Drag the σ slider to watch the continuous-limit BS curve morph.
The discrete binomial price (11) stays anchored; the BS curve (≈10.45) is a sourced reference, display-only.
Continue when you are done exploring.

beats.prompt: Same parameters: u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1, S₀=100. The stock now has 3 periods and 8 price paths. How many paths lead to the single top node (three consecutive up-moves), and what is the terminal stock price there?

interaction.scenario: Same parameters: u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1, S₀=100. The stock now has 3 periods and 8 price paths. How many paths lead to the single top node (three consecutive up-moves, uuu), and what is the terminal stock price there?

fields.label: Terminal price at the top node

feedback.correct: C(3,3) = 1 path leads to the top node (every period goes up: uuu). Terminal stock price = 100·(6/5)³ = 100·216/125 = 864/5 = 172.8 (#20).
hints: At the top node after 3 steps the stock went up every single period — how many ways can that happen?
Every period must go up; the only path is u·u·u → C(3,3) = 1. And the terminal price is S₀·u³ = 100·(6/5)³.

beats.prompt: Mastery — two parts. Part A: the 2-step backward fold and node weights. Part B: path-count vs q-weight discrimination.

interaction.scenario: Part A: S₀=100, u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1, K=100, 2-step call — give the intermediate node values C_u and C_d, the three terminal q-weights (k=2, k=1, k=0), and the fair call price. Part B: the middle node has __ paths leading to it, and a q-weight of __. Discriminate path count from q-probability.

feedback.correct: Backward fold: C_u = ½·44 + ½·0 = 22; C_d = ½·0 + ½·0 = 0. Node weights: k=2 → C(2,2)·(1/2)² = 1/4; k=1 → C(2,1)·(1/4) = 1/2; k=0 → C(2,0)·(1/2)² = 1/4. Check: 1/4+1/2+1/4=1 ✓. Price = ½·22 + ½·0 = 11 (#17). The middle node has C(2,1)=2 paths (ud and du) — a count. Its q-weight is C(2,1)·q¹·(1−q)¹ = 2·¼ = 1/2. Count answers 'how many ways'; weight answers 'how likely' (#18).
hints: Start at the terminal payoffs (144→44, 96→0, 64→0); fold backward using q=1/2 to get C_u and C_d; then fold again to today; weights = nCk·q^k·(1−q)^(n−k). Middle node: how many distinct sequences of ups/downs lead there?

C_u = ½·44 + ½·0 = 22; C_d = 0; top weight = C(2,2)·(1/4)=1/4; mid weight = C(2,1)·(1/4)=1/2; bot weight = 1/4. Paths to middle: ud and du → count = 2; each path has probability q·(1−q) = ¼; total q-weight = 2·¼ = 1/2.

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: 'Price a multi-step call by folding the tree backward — q-weighting each branch and discounting by R at every step. Node weights are Pascal's nCk·q^k(1−q)^(n−k) (they sum to 1). As the steps shrink to zero the discrete price converges to Black-Scholes c = S·N(d₁) − K·e^(−rT)·N(d₂) — reached with a slider but never graded here, because N(·) is irrational. The graded price is the exact binomial one (11).'
beats.prompt: Price any multi-step call by folding the tree backward — q-weighting each branch and discounting by R at every step.

feedback.correct: Node weights nCk·q^k(1−q)^(n−k) (they sum to 1). As the steps shrink to zero the discrete price converges to Black-Scholes c = S·N(d₁) − K·e^(−rT)·N(d₂) — reached with a slider but never graded here, because N(·) is irrational. Next: delta hedging and the Greeks.
hints: Read the recap card.
Fold backward: q-weight each branch, discount by R, repeat. Node weights are Pascal-counted and sum to 1.
Tap to finish.
